en.extract:
The white-eyed robin (Pachycephalopsis poliosoma) is a medium-sized bird weighing between 35 and 42 grams and measuring around 15 to 16.5 centimeters in length.  It is found in Indonesia and Papua New Guinea. 


Habitat
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forest. It lives in the hills and montane forests of New Guinea, where it prefers areas with lots of plants and trees. These spots provide good cover and plenty of food. The white-eyed robin often sits on low branches, where it monitors the ground, watching for prey. Its habitat is similar to that of other robins, such as the blue-gray (Thraupis episcopus) and smoky robins (Peneothello cryptoleuca). Each tends to stick to a specific level within the forest, minimizing competition. The white-eyed robin shares its habitat with many other species, showing how adaptable it is. These forests vary greatly in temperature and elevation, requiring flexibility.


Description
It is dark grey with a black eye-stripe, a brown tail and a white throat. Its bill is short and its eyes are brown. It has a strong, sturdy body with gray feathers that are slightly lighter on the belly. Its face and wings are darker, which makes its pale eyes stand out. Its eyes contrast against its dark facial features and gray throat, making it easy to recognize.


Behavior
The white-eyed robin mainly eats insects, and it hunts using a "sit-and-wait" approach. It perches quietly on a low branch, watching for any movement that could mean food. When it has the chance, it eats eggs or nestlings. 
The white-eyed robin is known best for its unique sound, which starts as a loud, raspy whistle.As it continues, the sounds become rougher and quicker, possibly reflecting excitement or agitation. Its loud whistle is used to call a mate or warn others to stay away from its territory. Its recognizable call helps the bird communicate within its environment and even alert others to danger.


Conservation status
The white-eyed robin faces no urgent threats. However, its habitat is at risk due to deforestation and other human activities.

The '''white-eyed robin ''(Pachycephalopsis poliosoma)''''' is a medium-sized bird weighing between 35 and 42 grams and measuring around 15 to 16.5 centimeters in length'''.''' <ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Boles |first=Walter |date=2020 |title=White-eyed Robin (Pachycephalopsis poliosoma), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/wherob1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.wherob1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is found in [[Indonesia]] and [[Papua New Guinea]]. 

== Habitat ==
Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].<ref name=IUCN>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Pachycephalopsis poliosoma'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22704920A93990892 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22704920A93990892.en |access-date=15 November 2021}}</ref> It lives in the hills and montane forests of [[New Guinea]], where it prefers areas with lots of plants and trees.<ref name="IUCN" /> These spots provide good cover and plenty of food.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=White-eyed Robin - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/wherob1 |access-date=2024-10-15 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> The white-eyed robin often sits on low branches, where it monitors the ground, watching for prey.<ref name=":0" /> Its habitat is similar to that of other robins, such as the [[Blue-gray tanager|blue-gray]] (''Thraupis episcopus'') and [[Smoky robin|smoky robins]] (''Peneothello cryptoleuca'').<ref name=":0" /> Each tends to stick to a specific level within the forest, minimizing competition.<ref name="IUCN" /> The white-eyed robin shares its habitat with many other species, showing how adaptable it is.<ref name=":0" /> These forests vary greatly in temperature and elevation, requiring flexibility.<ref name="IUCN" />

== Description ==
It is dark grey with a black eye-stripe, a brown tail and a white throat. Its bill is short and its eyes are brown. It has a strong, sturdy body with gray feathers that are slightly lighter on the belly.<ref name=":0" /> Its face and wings are darker, which makes its pale eyes stand out.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=White-eyed Robin (Pachycephalopsis poliosoma) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22704920 |access-date=2024-10-15 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref> Its eyes contrast against its dark facial features and gray throat, making it easy to recognize.<ref name="IUCN" />

== Behavior ==
The white-eyed robin mainly eats insects, and it hunts using a "sit-and-wait" approach.<ref name=":0" /> It perches quietly on a low branch, watching for any movement that could mean food.<ref name=":1" /> When it has the chance, it eats eggs or nestlings.<ref name=":0" /> 

The white-eyed robin is known best for its unique sound, which starts as a loud, raspy whistle.<ref name=":0" />As it continues, the sounds become rougher and quicker, possibly reflecting excitement or agitation.<ref name=":1" /> Its loud whistle is used to call a mate or warn others to stay away from its territory.<ref name="IUCN" /> Its recognizable call helps the bird communicate within its environment and even alert others to danger.<ref name=":0" />

== Conservation status ==
The white-eyed robin faces no urgent threats.<ref name=":2" /> However, its habitat is at risk due to [[deforestation]] and other human activities.<ref name="IUCN" />
